author: @Peaceful_Gojira
text:
Ever since watching this video back in 2017, it has helped me make a more definitive opinion and growth towards what I like/dislike in games. It's matured my thinking on not just "WOW THING GO BOOM, TAKE MY MONEY" no, now it's more about "Ok what does this game feature for gameplay loops, how long is the story/campaign, where can I go in this world, how are the graphics, can I pet the doggy, is there artificial or skill-based difficulty and if so, how big is the learning curve, is it unfair, did it launch in Cyberpunk 2077 states of broken/hype, etc."

If I hadn't watched this video, I probably wouldn't own Valheim, or Raft, or MGSV: TPP, Shadow Warrior 2, Metro 2033/Last Light Redux & Exodus, Alien Isolation, Slay the Spire, Destiny 2, and so much more....and that's just PC.

The big thing to take away from this video is simple: be your own videogame critic; make up your own mind but be receptive to others' perspectives (take that one to the bank kiddos). It's ok to align with some other like-minded reviewers, but at the end of the day, don't just be a moth to the flame with everyone else: be unique! Ensure that your thoughts and hypothesis aligns accurately and isn't just some hate/love-train that EVERYONE is on because it's popular, or because the whole: 
"Nope! You are nitpicking and biased. I win you lose, bye bye!"

So thanks dunkey. I like to come back to this vid (among others on your channel) to remind me that sometimes, you just gotta think hard on things, research, and eventually make up your own damn mind on things!

Cheers everyone.

author: @Benny_Blue
text:
Y’know what scares me, in a good way, about Dunkey: He’s very good at pairing legitimate points of criticism with _incredibly_ on-target, entertaining examples. His ending point in this video is “The language and points raised in reviews don’t always match the verdicts.” But that’s not what you take away, is it? What you take away is what the comments are *full* of — “IGN absolute *roasts* a game, then gives it a great score anyway.” 
His skill as a reviewer, and as an entertainer, is finding — or creating — these specific ideas that people *latch* onto, and become memes of themselves that are still indicative of the whole — like, to name another, Knack.

author: @osidiusemphatic
text:
A new part in this series would be fascinating. As social discourse has skewed cultural discourse, reviews have become grounds for tribalism, and sites like IGN absolutely feed that fact for engagement. I don't think they'd post reviews beyond a score these days otherwise.

On another note, that final point is what I believe ultimately defines whether a critic is 'good' or not. Discussion of flaws should be contextualised in the greater discussion, rather than being their own points, unless they are particularly egregious. When I was a film critic, I had a publicist tell me the reason they always invited me to screenings and gave me interview access, despite the fact I worked for a small site that covered a wide range of topics, was because my articles reacted to the work without getting emotional. That Mario review does the opposite in the worst way. The guy lets his frustrations shape the review, then ends with a reaction to the work that reflects the zeitgeist (all Mario = good Mario) rather than his actual opinion. And he got paid for it, presumably. It's not only lazy work, it's entirely without value.

author: @PraosTapeinos
text: Being in corporate America, I have some insight into how professional businesses understand a 1-10 rating scale. Interestingly enough, based on typical human psychology, rating systems go like this: 10 is perfect, 9 is good, 8 is alright, 1-7 is bad. If your company gets a 7 average on a customer survey, that’s considered awful. This is because people are relatively generous in the scores they give. It’s interesting that when Dunkey says that all IGN reviews are somewhere from 7-9 and that they all mean good, that’s because Dunkey’s 1-5 rating system is more logical. I point this out because he might be surprised to learn that 7-9 are not good scores according to most people. That being said, I’m not defending these critics, I just thought that was interesting.

author: @jackmoffat7417
text:
If you look at 4:04, you see that the score was 9.0, and the words 'It's okay' were shown. A 9/10 should not be 'okay.' A 9/10 should mean that the game is close to a masterpiece, but has a few flaws holding it back. For example, Breath of the Wild is 9/10 for me. I've put in 170 hours into the game, and I've enjoyed it a lot. The world is packed with things to do, amazing physics engine, combat is fun and versatile, and it contain that amazing sense of wonder and exploration that I want from a Zelda game. However, it has a few flaws. It rains too much, making climbing very frustrating. The weapon system, while it does challenge the player to go out of their comfort zone and is a wise design choice for the game, can be a little frustrating.  I feel like a 10-20% increase of durability all around would make the game much more manageable. The story even with the addition of VA and some memorable characters, falls just short due to it largely told through flashbacks in random order, making certain parts seem irrelevant. Why do I care if Zelda is torn between being a princess and a scholar? All I need to know is that she's fighting Ganon in the castle and I need to help her. These flaws are not huge, but they are enough to hold it back from true 10/10 status. 

I feel like reviewers, even many individual YouTubers at times, will be reluctant to give a score below a 7, as mentioned in the video. If a game isn't fun to play, is filled with bugs, or has terrible music and visuals(those aren't the most important parts of a game to me, but they do matter) then it's not getting above a 7. That's why dunkey's five point system is great. Amazing games get 4s or 5s, while average games that are still fun will normally get a 3, maybe 2. Bad games get 1 or 0. It's simple and expressed exactly what I need to know about a game. 

Also, I saw that the prepaid DLC was a pro in the review. What has the planet come to.

author: @AENock
text:
I remember when a 7/10 game could be a contender for GOTY. Now, in the year of our LORD twenty-one-seven if a game does not score a 9.5/10 or higher it will be forever destined to live at the bottom of the bargain bin at Walmart.

I can certainly understand the plight of the developer - we need this game to do well because we are asking our consumers to spend $40,$50,$60+ on our software. 

This is why marketing is such a large chunk of budget - you want as many eyes on these "9.5/10", "Masterful", "Timeless" buzzwords that correlate a good product.

You get that by giving early access to organizations such as IGN, who can get a lot of eyes on your product, and who might give an honest review, but sometimes its painfully obvious that they were bought.  

This is why I enjoy YouTube reviews of all kinds. You get a pretty honest and nuanced perspective, and like Dunkald said - you get a consistency that these mass media websites simply cannot offer anymore.

author: @Saizares
text: I just have but one point to make.... Game reviewers don't know either who's point of view the actual review really is. I think it works like this in IGN / Gamespot / etc. First guy writes what someone says in a video. Second guy says what the first guy wrote. Third guy takes a wheel, spins it around and the number that it stops at is the actual rating(IGN for example knows only the numbers that are 7 and higher). And after that of course the fourth guy actually decides for which game the actual review is.

author: @gusonian8637
text: I think a big reason that the language of a review often doesn’t line up with the verdict is because while a critical perspective of a game will often point out its flaws, most games are still pretty fun. Unless the game is broken or just painfully bad, you probably will still have fun playing it, which means that the reviewer bases the score off their impression of a game. It’s like watching a YouTube video - you might dislike the style or topic or whatever, but you probably still enjoyed it a bit and you’ll probably like the video.

author: @jesterdayplays771
text: I think the problem with Crash N Sane Trilogy is that Vicarious Visions probably based their remake on either Crash Bandicoot 2 or 3 and made all levels through that blueprint while Naughty Dog made three separate games originally. Why this could be a problem? Because in Crash 2 and 3, you have sliding jump and high jump options with circle/R1 while you don't have those options in the first game. It feels like Naughty Dog made Crash's normal jump reach longer in the first game than the sequels because Crash didn't have other jumping options in the first game. Vicarious Visions probably took Crash's moves from either 2 and 3 and copy pasted it to other games, while not adjusting the first game for a bit more longer jump reach. This becomes quite apparent in the levels Road to Nowhere and The High Road. Originals versions of those levels were nowhere near hard as their remade versions.

author: @Trips57
text:
To tell you guys the truth as 17876 comment here, if I love particular genre of games, I will trust specialist that have played 100 games in that genre than some popular reviewer that (while good and keen on things) just doesn't have both experience and knowledge required to go deeply into mechanics, similarities or weak sides. Dunky is fine because he hes unique look at games and even if I dislike his point of view at times, I am really glad he is doing his job and provides solid arguments. 

Main issue all of us have is that we treat attack on things we like or love as attack on us (and our tastes) which is very human, but extremely fucking stupid. Just because someone dislike your favourite song, food or color doesn't mean you are worse because of that. You dislike tons of things other ppl love and you don't give a flying shit about that. 

All of us would be just a tiny little bit happier if we could simply enjoy other ppl hard work without inserting our own ego everywhere. 

3rd beer coming right up.

Cheers.
